entry.title: Re: Report for Sales Order Value vs Billing Amount (Open...
entry.content:
Hi,

This requirement is currently not available as a standard app in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Standard analytical apps such as Sales Volume and Flexible Analysis are primarily based on billing (invoiced) data, whereas apps like Manage Sales Orders focus on sales order data only. Due to this separation, there is no standard report that directly compares Sales Order Value vs Billing Amount to determine open (unbilled) sales value.

In your scenario (order-related billing without deliveries), this behavior is expected, as sales and billing data are exposed through separate analytical models (CDS views) and are not combined in standard content.

To meet this requirement, the recommended approach is to create a Custom CDS View or Custom Analytical Query, where:

Sales order item data is used for ordered value
Billing document data is used for billed amount
Both are linked via the reference between billing documents and sales orders
A calculated field is created to derive the open sales value

If this functionality is important for your business and you would prefer it as part of standard SAP content, you may consider submitting a feature request via KBA

2963059 - How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

This allows SAP Product Management to evaluate the requirement for future releases.

Best regards,

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: API: Read and Modify WBS element Status
entry.content:
Dear @XANDOGAN,

No, customer-defined project/WBS user statuses such as Z001, Z002, etc. cannot be created or maintained as standard configuration in Public Cloud. The classic PS status profile configuration known from on-premise/private cloud, such as transaction OK02 for user status profile definition is not available as a customer configuration option in Public Cloud. SAP confirms that the user status for projects/WBS elements in S/4HANA Cloud cannot be influenced by the user and is used internally by the system to prevent specific business transactions

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2810998

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: AI Agents Are Now a Board-Level Risk. Is Your Governance Ready?
entry.content:
The board conversation about AI has shifted. It used to be about ambition: which use cases to pursue, how quickly to scale, whether the organization was moving fast enough. Increasingly, it's about accountability. What agents are running across the enterprise, what they're costing, whether they're delivering returns, and what happens when something goes wrong. 

Most CIOs are not yet ready for that conversation. 

The reason is structural. AI agents have proliferated faster than governance has followed. Teams across the business have deployed agents independently, on multiple platforms, for multiple purposes, with varying degrees of oversight. The result resembles the early shadow IT problem: ungoverned assets, unclear ownership, untracked costs. But there's a critical difference. Agents act autonomously. They access data, execute decisions, and interact with other systems without a human in the loop. The risk profile is not the same as an unauthorized SaaS subscription. It's considerably higher. 

Three questions define the board-level governance challenge. 

The first is compliance. Regulatory frameworks, including the EU AI Act, require organizations to maintain an inventory of AI systems in use, document associated risks, and demonstrate control. Meeting that bar requires architecture context, documentation workflows, and cross-platform visibility. Most organizations do not yet have the infrastructure to confidently answer an auditor's questions. 

The second is returns. Deployment count is not a measure of value. The organizations that can answer the ROI question are those that have connected their agents to the business processes they were meant to improve and can show whether those processes actually changed. Without that linkage, AI investment is an act of faith. 

The third is control. Knowing which agents are running is the floor, not the ceiling. Control means knowing who owns each agent, what it accesses, whether it is authorized to run, and what the continuity plan is if it fails. It means governance enforced at runtime, not documented after the fact. 

The SAP AI Agent Hub is built to give CIOs confident answers to all three. It provides a single, vendor-agnostic view of the entire agent landscape, grounded in the architecture and business process context needed to move from inventory to accountability. 

The CIOs who will lead on AI are the ones investing in governance infrastructure now, before the problem becomes expensive to fix. That's the lesson shadow IT already taught us.

entry.title: create identity column
entry.content:
Hi, ALL,

I have installed 16.1 version of SAP ASE.

Then I started the dbisql and tried the following:

 

CREATE TABLE leagues(id integer identity primary key, name varchar(100), drafttype integer, scoringtype integer, roundvalues integer, leaguetype char(5), salary integer, benchplayers integer);

 

This command gave an error.

 

Then I tried:

 

CREATE TABLE leagues(id int identity primary key, name varchar(100), drafttype integer, scoringtype integer, roundvalues integer, leaguetype char(5), salary integer, benchplayers integer);

 

and that succeeded.

 

Shouldn't the first succeed as well?

 

Then I tried the following:

 

INSERT INTO leagues VALUES(1,'Demo - Roto (Auction)',1,1,1,'demo',260,0);

 

That failed with the explanation that I need to supply the field list.

So then I tried:

 

INSERT INTO leagues(id, name, drafttype, scoringtype, roundvalues, leaguetype, salary, benchplayers) VALUES(1,'Demo - Roto (Auction)',1,1,1,'demo',260,0);

That also failed.

However doing:

 

INSERT INTO leagues(name, drafttype, scoringtype, roundvalues, leaguetype, salary, benchplayers) VALUES('Demo - Roto (Auction)',1,1,1,'demo',260,0);

 

succeeded.

Shouldn't the second INSERT succeed as well?

 

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: How do I create a JDBC data source for an on-premises AS400 (DB2) and which database type Can I
entry.content:
We are currently working on establishing JDBC connectivity between SAP CPI and the DB2 AS400 (IBM iSeries)(On premsie-conecting via cloud connector) system as part of the current integration requirement.

While configuring the JDBC connection, we are encountering connectivity and driver-related issues during connection testing from SAP CPI.

From our initial analysis and online references, we observed that SAP CPI standard JDBC support for DB2 AS400 (IBM i) may have certain limitations compared to standard DB2 LUW databases.

We need confirmation on the following points:

  1. Whether SAP CPI officially supports DB2 AS400 / IBM iSeries connectivity.
  2. Which JDBC driver should be used for this setup.
  3. Whether any additional configuration is required from CPI / BTP / Cloud Connector side.
  4. Whether similar integrations were previously implemented in SAP Integration Suite.
  5. Whether there are any known restrictions or recommended approaches for AS400 connectivity.

Currently, we are testing using the below JDBC URL formats:

  • jdbc:db2://virtual host:virtual port/databasename

Any guidance, documentation, supported approach, or confirmation from SAP would help us proceed further.

Thanks,
Praveen

entry.title: How to derive the Sold-to Party on COGS Split postings in Intercompany flows
entry.content:
We are running SAP S/4HANA with Account-Based CO-PA (Margin Analysis) for a food manufacturing company (0408 UK). We deliver products manufactured in France (company code 0055) to UK final customers (Customer UK 1, Customer UK 2...) through an intercompany flow via company code 0408 (UK)

PROBLEM: The COGS detail accounts (971XXX ) generated by the COGS Split during goods issue (operation type COIE) do NOT have the correct customer in CO-PA.

They are posted with the intercompany customer (S0408) instead of the final sold-to party (Customer UK 1, Customer UK 2....) which makes the customer margin reporting incorrect in KE30.

We need the 971xxx accounts to be assigned to the SOLD-TO PARTY (VBAK-KUNNR) from the sales order, so that customer margin reporting in CO-PA (KE30) shows correct COGS by final customer.

 

QUESTIONS
 Is there a standard config (or ABAP) or derivation  for this use case? And the  Best approach for historical  fix ?

entry.title: Re: What Happens to Your Data When You Enable SAP's AI Features?
entry.content:
Good blog post @jaripie! in my opinion a very underrated topic! I think the answer and possibilities depend on the SAP product....some come with Joule out of the box and no activation or configuration is required, hence the also do not require the SAP AI Core service.

Even in SAP Cloud ERP (s/4Hana cloud private edition) you don't necessarily need the SAP AI Core service. Depending on the use cases, a lot of the require the basic ISLM setup. Here you can run the SAP AI core service in your BTP (sub)account or you use a SAP-managed instance....and usually each ISLM scenario defined which model e.g. GPT-4o has to be used.

In my opinion the announced SAP Business AI Platform would be the perfect place to have some central configuration options around privacy (e.g. masking) or whitelisting and exclusion of certain LLMs and an audit log to see what was actually send to which model. After all each customer might have different requirements...

 

PS Your first link refers to the orchestration workflow v1 which is deprecated!

entry.title: Re: How to implement Organization Rule to filter out false-positives in SOD Risk Analysis in GRC Acc
entry.content:
Thank you Akshay J, very useful information provided.

Please find below an updated version including for example some additional information on Organization Rule Creation Wizard available in SAP AC v12 SP16+.:

Overview

Organizational Rules in SAP GRC Access Control are specialized filters designed to eliminate false positive Segregation of Duties (SoD) violations in access risk analysis reports. These rules enable organizations to account for organizational-level restrictions when evaluating access risks, ensuring that conflicts are only reported when they occur within the same organizational boundary — such as Company Code, Plant, Controlling Area, or Purchasing Organization.

The organization rules functionality provides an additional filter for SoD reports. Organization rules are used to eliminate false positive risks in access risk analysis reports. This functionality is intended for exception-based reporting only.

Note: Source (SAP Help, AC 12.0 SP27): 'The organization rules functionality provides an additional filter for your segregation of duties (SoD) reports. Organization rules are used to eliminate false positive risks in your access risk analysis reports. Use this functionality for exception-based reporting only.'

2. Objective

The primary objective of organizational rules is exception-based reporting: to remove false positives that result from organizational-level segregation, where users may have conflicting transactions but are restricted to different organizational values (e.g., different Company Codes, Plants, or Purchasing Organizations) and therefore cannot exercise the conflict within the same organizational context.

3. Scenario and Business Challenge

An organization has implemented a shared services model where finance teams are segregated by Company Code. The current SoD analysis shows false positive conflicts when users have vendor invoice posting rights (FB60) for Company Code 1008 and vendor master data maintenance rights (FK02) for Company Code 1000. While these are conflicting functions, they pose no actual risk since they operate on different organizational entities.

 

Figure 1: SoD Architecture – Risk ZP001_1 with Functions, Actions, Permissions, and Test Roles

4. Good Practices for Configuration

Organizations should implement organizational rules exclusively for exception-based reporting and only after thorough analysis to ensure that the situation genuinely warrants their use. The following critical considerations apply:

  • Exception-based only: Use organizational rules exclusively for exception-based reporting to remove false positive conflicts that result from organization-level segregation.
  • Timing: Do not institute organizational rules until the remediation phase of your GRC project. Only create rules after identifying a specific organizational rule scenario.
  • Avoid mass org-level reporting: Do not use organizational rules to group users into reports by organizational level for the purpose of distributing SoD reports to various management levels.
  • Performance impact: Due to the sizable performance impact that organizational rules can have, use them only in situations where the company has made a conscious decision to segregate via organization levels.
  • Risk of under-reporting: If organizational rules are configured incorrectly, you could potentially filter out too much. From a control perspective, it is much better to over-report (causing false positives) than under-report (causing false negatives).
  • Role assignment vs. org rules: Most organizations control data access through role assignment rather than organizational rules. This functionality should only be enabled for functions requiring explicit organizational segregation.

Caution: Caution (SAP Help, AC 12.0 SP27): 'If you create organizational rules incorrectly, you could potentially filter out too much. By filtering out too much, you cannot identify possible control concerns with your access. From a control perspective, it is much better to over-report (causing false positives) rather than under-report (causing false negatives).'

5. Prerequisites

Before creating organizational rules, ensure all of the following prerequisites are met:

  • Organizational rule scenario identified: You can create organization rules only after you have identified a possible organizational rule scenario (SAP Help, AC 12.0 SP10).
  • Rule set contains org-relevant authorization objects: The SoD functions and risks must include authorization objects with org-level fields (e.g., F_LFA1_BUK / BUKRS for Company Code, M_EINF_EKO / EKORG for Purchasing Org). Without the relevant field in the rule set permission, the org rule will have no effect on filtering.
  • $-variable placeholders used in function permissions: The permission definitions within functions must use $BUKRS (or the relevant $-variable) as the Value From. This enables the runtime substitution that org rules rely on.
  • Repository synchronization is current: Run GRAC_REPOSITORY_OBJECT_SYNC (via SA38) to ensure that user, role, and profile assignments — including their organizational level values — are up to date in the GRC repository. Stale repository data will prevent accurate org rule filtering.
  • System (RFC connector) is configured: The back-end systems being analysed must be defined as RFC connectors in GRC. The Systems tab on the org rule links the rule to specific connectors.
  • (Optional) Configuration parameter 1032 verified: Navigate to SPRO → SAP GRC → Access Control → Maintain Configuration Settings and verify that parameter 1032 (Use Organization Rule) is set to 'Yes'. This parameter may be automatically enabled in newer service packs — only check this if org rules are not functioning as expected after following all other steps.
  • GRC project phase: Organizational rules should not be created before the remediation phase of the GRC implementation.

 

Figure 2: SPRO – SAP GRC Maintain Configuration Settings (Parameter 1032 highlighted) [Illustrative]

6. Step-by-Step Implementation

The following steps walk through a complete end-to-end implementation of an organizational rule for the sample scenario: eliminating the false positive SoD conflict between Vendor Invoice Posting (FB60 / Company Code 1008) and Vendor Master Maintenance (FK02 / Company Code 1000).

  Step 1:  Identify and Define the SoD Risk

Identify the specific SoD risk for which the organizational rule should apply. In this example, risk ZP001_1 'Vendor Master Maintenance vs. Process Vendor Invoices' covers two conflicting functions:

  • ZAP02_1 – Process Vendor Invoices (Action: FB60, Permission: F_LFA1_BUK / BUKRS = $BUKRS)
  • ZPR01_1 – Vendor Master Maintenance (Action: FK02, Permission: F_LFA1_BUK / BUKRS = $BUKRS)

Note: $BUKRS in SAP GRC ruleset permission files is a variable placeholder that represents the Company Code field (BUKRS) in SAP authorization objects. When the ruleset is evaluated, $BUKRS gets populated with actual Company Code values from the user's authorizations or role assignments. This is what allows the org rule to compare actual values at analysis time rather than generic wildcards.

Risk: ZP001_1

 

Figure 3: Access Risk ZP001_1 – Risk Definition with Functions ZAP02_1 and ZPR01_1

Function: ZAP02_1 – Process Vendor Invoices

Action tab:

 

Figure 4: Function ZAP02_1 – Action tab (FB60: Enter Incoming Invoices)

Permission tab:

 

Figure 5: Function ZAP02_1 – Permission tab (F_LFA1_BUK / BUKRS = $BUKRS)

Function: ZPR01_1 – Vendor Master Maintenance

Action tab:

 

Figure 6: Function ZPR01_1 – Action tab (FK02: Change Vendor Accounting)

Permission tab:

 

Figure 7: Function ZPR01_1 – Permission tab (F_LFA1_BUK / BUKRS = $BUKRS)

  Step 2:  Assign Roles to a Test User and Run Repository Synchronization

Create or identify two SAP roles with different Company Code assignments, assign them to a test user, and then run the repository synchronization so GRC captures the current access landscape:

Role

Transaction

Company Code (BUKRS)

Authorization Object

Z_TEST_ROLE_1008

FB60

1008

F_LFA1_BUK

Z_TEST_ROLE_1000

FK02

1000

F_LFA1_BUK

Role 1: Z_TEST_ROLE_1008 – FB60 / Company Code 1008

 

Figure 8: Role Z_TEST_ROLE_1008 – Authorization details showing BUKRS = 1008

Role 2: Z_TEST_ROLE_1000 – FK02 / Company Code 1000

 

Figure 9: Role Z_TEST_ROLE_1000 – Authorization details showing BUKRS = 1000

After assigning both roles to the test user (e.g., TEST_SEC), run the repository synchronization to refresh GRC's local copy of authorization data:

  • Transaction: SA38 → Program: GRAC_REPOSITORY_OBJECT_SYNC
  • This ensures the GRC repository reflects the current Company Code values assigned to each role and user.

 

Figure 10: GRAC_REPOSITORY_OBJECT_SYNC – Repository Object Synchronization Execution [Illustrative]

Caution: Schedule GRAC_REPOSITORY_OBJECT_SYNC as a recurring background job. Without current repository data, organizational rule filtering will not reflect the latest user and role assignments. Any changes to roles (adding or removing Company Codes) require a re-sync before the org rule produces accurate results.

 

  Step 3:  Generate Access Control Rules (Before Creating Org Rule)

Before creating the organizational rule, regenerate the Access Control rules to ensure the rule set incorporates any changes made to functions or risks:

  • Transaction: SA38 → Program: GRAC_GENERATE_RULES
  • Select the relevant Rule Set (or leave blank to regenerate all).
  • Execute in foreground for small sets; use background for large rule sets.

 

Figure 11: GRAC_GENERATE_RULES – Rule Generation Execution and Log [Illustrative]

Caution: Always run GRAC_GENERATE_RULES after any of the following: (1) creating or modifying a Function, (2) creating or modifying a Risk, (3) creating or modifying an Organizational Rule. Without rule regeneration, changes will not be reflected in risk analysis results.

  Step 4a:  Create the Organizational Rule – Manual Method

Navigate to the Organization Rules setup screen in SAP NetWeaver Business Client (NWBC):

  • Path: Setup → Exception Access Rules → Organization Rules → Create

Complete all required fields using the reference table below:

Field

Value / Setting

Notes

Organization Rule ID *

ZORGRULE1

10-character alphanumeric; no spaces allowed. Use underscores (_). Must be unique.

Parent OrgRule ID

(leave blank for new rules)

Optional. Used to create hierarchical rule structures. If specified, this rule inherits from the parent. Note: The Systems tab cannot be modified on child rules (inherited from parent).

Risk ID *

ZP001_1

Select from the list of available Risk IDs. Each org rule applies to exactly one Risk ID.

Description *

Org Rule test 1

Enter a meaningful description that explains why this rule exists and what false positive it eliminates.

Org Level tab → Add

Company Code (BUKRS)

Select the organizational level dimension. Choose 'Company Code(BUK...)' for BUKRS-based segregation.

Value From

1000

Enter the first Company Code value. Repeat this row for each additional Company Code (e.g., 1008).

Value To

(blank or same as From)

Optional: Enter an end value to define a range of Company Code values. Leave blank for exact values.

Condition

AND

AND: All specified org level entries must apply. OR: Any one entry is sufficient. Use AND when both Company Codes must be present in the user's access for the rule to filter.

Status

Active

Must be set to Active for the rule to be applied during risk analysis.

Systems tab → Add

D00CLNT100 (RFC Connector)

Add the RFC connector system(s) to which this rule applies. Without a system entry, the rule will not filter in analyses scoped to a specific connector.

 

Figure 12: Organization Rule ZORGRULE1 – Org Level tab with Company Codes 1000 and 1008 (AND condition)

Systems Tab Configuration:

The Systems tab on the Organization Rule determines which back-end connector systems the rule is applied to. At least one system must be added for the rule to take effect during analyses run against that system.

 

Figure 13: Organization Rule – Systems Tab showing RFC connector assignments [Illustrative]

Note: AND vs OR Condition Logic: When multiple org-level rows are added to a rule with Condition = AND, the system requires that all listed Company Codes appear in the user's authorization assignments for the conflict to be filtered. OR condition means any one of the listed Company Codes is sufficient. In the scenario above, AND is correct: the user must hold both Company Code 1000 (FK02) AND Company Code 1008 (FB60) for the conflict to be recognized as a cross-org false positive.

Supported Organizational Level Dimensions

Organizational rules can be applied to any authorization object and field that is present in the rule set function permissions. Common organizational level fields include:

Org Level Field

SAP Authorization Object

Typical Use Case

BUKRS – Company Code

F_LFA1_BUK, F_BKPF_BUK

Segregate AP/AR processing by company

WERKS – Plant

M_MSEG_WMB, M_MSEG_BWA

Segregate goods movements by plant

EKORG – Purchasing Organization

M_EINF_EKO, M_BEST_EKO

Segregate purchasing activities by org

KOKRS – Controlling Area

K_CSKB, K_CSKS

Segregate cost center control by area

VKORG – Sales Organization

V_VBAK_VKO

Segregate sales order creation by org

PRCTR – Profit Center

K_PCA

Segregate profit center postings

 

  Step 4b:  Create Organizational Rules Using the Wizard (Alternative – SP16+)

From SAP Access Control 12.0 SP16 onwards, the Organization Rule Creation Wizard provides a guided, streamlined flow for creating one or more organizational rules efficiently. The wizard is particularly recommended for bulk rule creation, as it automates rule ID generation, system assignment, and basic validation — reducing configuration errors compared to manual creation.

Note: Source (SAP Help AC 12.0 SP16 – KG confirmed): https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ACCESS_CONTROL/5cae1bc9a72348389e91183714220e30/a09276518a95115de10000000a44176d.html  The wizard is available from SP16 onwards and is the SAP-recommended method for bulk org rule creation.

Navigation:

  • Path: Setup → Exception Access Rules → Organization Rules
  • Select 'Organization Rule Creation Wizard' (not the standard 'Create' button)

Wizard Stage 1 – Overview

The first screen provides an overview of the wizard steps. Review the instructions and select Next to continue.

 

Figure 13a: Wizard Stage 1 – Overview of all wizard steps [Illustrative]

Wizard Stage 2 – Select System and Rule Set

Choose the target ERP system connector and one or more Rule Sets from which organization levels will be extracted. Multiple Rule Sets can be selected using Ctrl+click.

  • Select the ERP System (RFC connector) against which org values will be queried
  • Select the Rule Set(s) — multiple selections supported via Ctrl+click
  • Only rule sets that contain org-relevant authorization fields will yield org rule candidates

 

Figure 13b: Wizard Stage 2 – System and Rule Set selection [Illustrative]

Wizard Stage 3 – Review Organization Levels

The wizard automatically extracts Organization Levels from the risks in the selected Rule Set. Review the list and select the organizational dimensions you want to use for filtering (e.g., Company Code / BUKRS).

  • Only org-level fields that exist in the rule set's function permissions will appear
  • Deselect any dimensions that are not relevant for the current org rule scenario

Wizard Stage 4 – Select Org Values

The wizard connects to the selected ERP system (via RFC) and retrieves available org values for each selected dimension. Choose the specific values that define the segregation boundary:

  • Select the Company Codes (or other org values) that apply to the segregation scenario
  • Remove any values that should not be included in the org rule scope
  • The wizard will generate one org rule entry per selected value

Caution: The wizard queries the connected ERP system in real time for org values. Ensure the RFC connector is active and the repository sync is current before running the wizard, or values may be missing or stale.

Wizard Stage 5 – Review Organization Rules

The wizard presents a preview of all org rules that will be created. At this stage you can:

  • Review each generated rule (Rule ID, Risk ID, Org Level, Value From, Condition)
  • Set the 'Organization Rule Format' field to add a common prefix (e.g., ZORGRULE_) to all generated Rule IDs. This makes rules easier to sort, identify, and manage in large environments.
  • Remove any rules that should not be created

 

Figure 13c: Wizard Stage 5 – Review Rules with Organization Rule Format prefix [Illustrative]

Note: Performance Tip: Use the Organization Rule Format prefix to group related rules by scenario. For example: ZBUKRS_ for company code rules, ZWERKS_ for plant rules. This makes it easy to filter, report, and deactivate related rules as a group.

Wizard Stage 6 – Generate

Select Generate (or Finish) to complete rule creation. The wizard creates all reviewed org rules in the system and confirms the count of rules created. After generation completes:

  • Run GRAC_GENERATE_RULES to incorporate the new org rules into the rule set (mandatory)
  • Verify the created rules in Setup → Exception Access Rules → Organization Rules

Manual Creation (Step 4a)

Wizard Creation (Step 4b)

One rule created per execution

Multiple rules created in a single wizard run

Full control over every field

Automated field population (Rule ID, System, Org Level)

Suitable for 1–3 specific rules

Recommended for 4+ rules or bulk initial setup

Available from AC 12.0 SP10

Available from AC 12.0 SP16 onwards

Org Rule Format prefix: manual

Org Rule Format prefix: applied automatically to all generated rules

 

  Step 5:  Regenerate Rules After Creating the Organizational Rule

After saving the organizational rule, the rule set must be regenerated to incorporate the new org rule into the analysis engine:

  • Transaction: SA38 → Program: GRAC_GENERATE_RULES
  • Select the same Rule Set used in your risk analysis.
  • Execute and verify that the log confirms the org rule was processed.

Caution: This step is mandatory. Skipping rule regeneration after creating or modifying an org rule means the rule will not be applied during subsequent risk analyses, even if 'Consider Org Rule' is checked.

 

  Step 6:  Run Risk Analysis WITHOUT Considering Org Rule (Baseline)

Before enabling the org rule, run a baseline risk analysis to observe the default behavior. This establishes the false positive baseline that the org rule will eliminate:

  • Path: Access Management → Access Risk Analysis → User Level
  • Set User = TEST_SEC, Access Risk ID = ZP001_1, Rule Set = Z custom ruleset 1
  • Report Options: Format = Detail, Type = Access Risk Analysis, also check Permission Level
  • Additional Criteria: Leave 'Consider Org Rule' UNCHECKED
  • Run in Foreground

Risk Analysis Input – Without Org Rule:

 

Figure 14: Risk Analysis Criteria – 'Consider Org Rule' unchecked (baseline run)

Results – Without Org Rule (BUKRS shown as $BUKRS):

 

Figure 15: Risk Analysis Results – Value From column shows $BUKRS (unresolved variable = false positive baseline)

Note: When org rules are NOT considered, the Value From column in the results displays $BUKRS. This is because the analysis engine has not yet resolved the variable placeholder to actual Company Code values from the repository. These rows represent potential conflicts that may be false positives — they cannot yet be confirmed as real risks without knowing the actual org values.

 

  Step 7:  Run Risk Analysis WITH Considering Org Rule

Now run the same analysis with the 'Consider Org Rule' option enabled to see the filtering effect:

  • Path: Access Management → Access Risk Analysis → User Level
  • Same Analysis Criteria as above (User, Risk ID, Rule Set)
  • Additional Criteria: Check 'Consider Org Rule'
  • Run in Foreground

Risk Analysis Input – With Org Rule:

 

Figure 16: Risk Analysis Criteria – 'Consider Org Rule' checked (org rule filtering enabled)

Results – With Org Rule (BUKRS resolved to actual values):

 

Figure 17: Risk Analysis Results – Value From column shows actual Company Codes 1000/1008; Org Rule ID = ZORGRULE1

Note: With 'Consider Org Rule' enabled, the Value From column now displays actual Company Code values (1000 and 1008) instead of $BUKRS. The Org Rule ID column (ZORGRULE1) confirms the rule was applied. Since the user holds FB60 only for Company Code 1008 and FK02 only for Company Code 1000, the conflict is confirmed as a cross-organizational false positive. Auditors can now clearly see this distinction and justifiably exclude these rows from the risk report.

 

  Step 8:  Applying Org Rules at Role and Profile Level (Optional)

The 'Consider Org Rule' option is not limited to User Level analysis. It is also available in Role Level and Profile Level risk analyses. Use role-level analysis when evaluating access risks during role design or re-certification, before the roles are assigned to any user:

  • Path: Access Management → Access Risk Analysis → Role Level
  • Path: Access Management → Access Risk Analysis → Profile Level
  • In both cases, the 'Consider Org Rule' checkbox appears in Additional Criteria and functions identically.

 

Figure 18: Risk Analysis – Role Level with 'Consider Org Rule' checked [Illustrative]

Note: Role-level analysis with org rules is useful during the role design phase to verify that the proposed role structure will not generate false positive SoD conflicts once assigned to users. Profile-level analysis is typically used in legacy SAP ERP environments where access is managed via profiles rather than roles.

 

7. Organization Rule Lifecycle Management

Organizational rules require ongoing maintenance. The following operations are supported:

7.1 Modifying an Organizational Rule

Navigate to: Setup → Exception Access Rules → Organization Rules

Select the Organization Rule ID and choose Open. You can modify the following fields only (SAP Help, AC 12.0 SP11):

Tab

Modifiable Fields

Fields You Cannot Modify

Header

Parent OrgRule ID, Description

Organization Rule ID, Risk ID

Org Level

Value From, Value To, Condition, Status

Organization Level (dimension itself)

Systems

No changes allowed on parent org rules

System assignments of parent rules

 

Caution: After modifying an organizational rule, always run GRAC_GENERATE_RULES again to regenerate the rule set. Without regeneration, the modification will not take effect in risk analyses.

 

7.2 Deleting an Organizational Rule

Navigate to: Setup → Exception Access Rules → Organization Rules

  • Select the Organization Rule ID in the list.
  • Choose Delete.
  • Confirm the deletion prompt.
  • Run GRAC_GENERATE_RULES after deletion to update the rule set.

Caution: Deleting an organizational rule that is currently referenced in saved analysis variants or scheduled background jobs may cause those jobs to fail or produce unexpected results. Review all saved variants and scheduled jobs before deletion. Reference: SAP Help AC 12.0 SP25 – Deleting an Organization Rule: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ACCESS_CONTROL/5cae1bc9a72348389e91183714220e30/58bce94fb0d04a59e10000000a445394.html

 

7.3 Organization Rule Creation Wizard – Reference

For a full step-by-step wizard walkthrough, refer to Step 4b of the implementation section above. The wizard is available from SAP Access Control 12.0 SP16 onwards and is the recommended approach for creating multiple org rules in a single session.

  • Path: Setup → Exception Access Rules → Organization Rules → Organization Rule Creation Wizard
  • SAP Help Reference (SP16): https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ACCESS_CONTROL/5cae1bc9a72348389e91183714220e30/a09276518a95115de10000000a44176d.html

8. Mitigated User for Organization Rules

A related concept is the Mitigated User for Organization Rules. This allows specific users to be exempted from an organizational rule — meaning the org rule will NOT filter their conflicts, ensuring that even cross-org risks remain visible in the report for those users:

  • Path: Access Management → Mitigation → User-Level Mitigations → Mitigated User for Org Rules
  • This is useful when a specific user is granted an exception and must remain under full SoD scrutiny regardless of org segregation.
  • The mitigated user concept is distinct from standard mitigation controls, which accept a risk with compensating controls.

Note: Do not confuse Organizational Rules with Mitigation Controls. Organizational rules filter what is reported (reducing false positives). Mitigation controls formally accept a risk and document the compensating control. Both mechanisms can co-exist for the same risk.

 

9. Troubleshooting

The following table covers the most common issues encountered when implementing organizational rules and their recommended resolutions:

Symptom

Likely Cause

Resolution / SAP Note

Org rule is not filtering any results even with 'Consider Org Rule' checked

GRAC_GENERATE_RULES was not run after creating the org rule

Run GRAC_GENERATE_RULES for the relevant rule set and re-run the analysis

Org rule is not filtering even after rule generation

The function permission does not contain the org-level field (e.g., BUKRS is missing from the permission)

Add the correct authorization object and field ($BUKRS) to the function permission and regenerate rules

Org rule is not filtering even though the field is present

GRAC_REPOSITORY_OBJECT_SYNC has not been run; GRC does not have current org-level values for the user/role

Run GRAC_REPOSITORY_OBJECT_SYNC and re-run the analysis

Results show $BUKRS instead of actual values even with org rule considered

The role does not set the Company Code via an org level ($BUKRS); it may use explicit values or wildcard '*'

Check the role authorization in PFCG. Ensure the role uses organizational levels ($BUKRS) not hardcoded values

Child org rule does not apply to additional systems

System assignments are inherited from the parent org rule and cannot be modified on child rules

Modify system assignments on the parent rule; or create an independent rule instead of a child rule

Risk analysis performance degrades significantly after enabling org rules

Too many org rules applied to high-volume users or connector groups

Limit org rule scope — apply only to specific users/roles that require org-level filtering. Avoid mass application

Background risk analysis job fails after deleting an org rule

Saved job variant still references the deleted org rule ID

Update or delete the saved variant in the Risk Analysis screen before re-scheduling the job

500 Internal Server Error occurs during wizard – rule set selection step

System error during ruleset retrieval in the wizard. Known defect in some SP versions.

Apply the correction described in SAP Note 3625382. Verify service pack patch level.

Wizard completes but does not generate the expected set of org rules

Multiple org level dimensions were combined in a single org rule instead of being separated

Re-run the wizard and separate each Organization Level into its own Org Rule. Alternatively, change the Condition from AND to OR if cross-dimension filtering is intended. See SAP Note 1514544 for additional guidance.

Organization values are missing or incomplete in the wizard value selection step

GRC repository data is not current — the wizard retrieves org values live from the connected ERP system

Run GRAC_REPOSITORY_OBJECT_SYNC before launching the wizard to ensure all org values are available.

 

10. Performance Optimization Guidance

Organizational rules can have a material impact on risk analysis performance when applied broadly. The following recommendations help balance control coverage with system performance:

Recommendation

Rationale

How to Implement

Use the Wizard for bulk rule creation

Reduces manual errors, ensures consistent formatting, and avoids missing system assignments

Setup → Exception Access Rules → Organization Rules → Organization Rule Creation Wizard (SP16+)

Apply Org Rule Format prefixes

Prefixes enable fast filtering and sorting in the org rule list, especially when 20+ rules exist

In Wizard Stage 5, set the 'Organization Rule Format' field (e.g., ZBUKRS_, ZWERKS_)

Consolidate similar org rules

Reduce total rule count by combining multiple similar org values into range entries (Value From / Value To)

In the Org Level tab, use Value To to define a range instead of creating one entry per value

Limit org rules to specific risks

Apply org rules only to risks where org-level segregation is explicitly documented and approved

Each org rule is scoped to one Risk ID — do not apply to all risks by default

Avoid applying org rules to all users via bulk analysis

Running user-level analysis for all users with org rules enabled multiplies processing load

Use org rules only in targeted analyses (specific user, role, or risk) or in background jobs during off-peak hours

Schedule background jobs during off-peak hours

Risk analyses with org rules are resource-intensive; background execution avoids UI timeouts

In the Risk Analysis screen, select 'Run in Background' and schedule via job management (SM37)

 

Note: Recommendation (SAP Help AC 12.0 SP27): 'Due to the sizable performance impact that organization rules can have, use them only in those situations where the company has made a conscious decision to segregate via organization levels.'

11. Limitations and Known Constraints

  • Single Risk per Org Rule: Each organizational rule can be linked to exactly one Risk ID. Create separate org rules for each risk that requires org-level filtering.
  • Not a substitute for role design: Organizational rules filter analysis output only. They do not change how authorizations are granted in the back-end system.
  • No effect on Critical Action / Critical Permission reports: The 'Consider Org Rule' option is only available for Access Risk Analysis type reports, not for Critical Action, Critical Permission, or Critical Role/Profile report types.
  • Org level field must exist in the rule set: The org rule only takes effect if the relevant authorization object and field ($BUKRS, etc.) is present in the function permission definition. Org rules cannot retroactively add org fields to existing rule sets.
  • Firefighter / Emergency Access: Org rules do not apply to Emergency Access (Firefighter) usage log analysis. Firefighter activity is reviewed through a separate process.
  • Derived roles and inheritance: For derived roles, ensure that the organizational level values ($BUKRS) are correctly maintained in PFCG. If derived roles use inherited parent values, the GRC repository may not capture individual org level assignments correctly.

12. Key Benefits and Conclusion

When implemented correctly and for the right use cases, organizational rules provide the following measurable benefits:

Benefit

Description

False Positive Elimination

Filters SoD conflicts that appear risky at the transaction level but are safe due to organizational restrictions — preventing reporting of violations when users have conflicting access across different organizational boundaries (e.g., different Company Codes, Plants, or Purchasing Organizations).

Precise Risk Tracking

Provides unique identification for each org rule (Rule ID), enabling targeted application to specific risks and granular control over which SoD risks consider org-level filtering.

Enhanced Audit Trail

Creates a clear reference system for documenting why certain risks were filtered. The Org Rule ID column in analysis results provides an auditable record of which rule was applied and why a conflict was filtered.

Regulatory Compliance Support

Supports justifiable risk exception documentation based on organizational controls — demonstrating to auditors (e.g., SOX, GDPR, DSGVO) that access risks are managed at the appropriate organizational granularity.

Reduced Noise in Reports

Minimizes remediation workload by ensuring the SoD team focuses only on genuine, actionable conflicts rather than organizational artifacts of a shared services or multi-company structure.

 

Note: Official SAP Help Portal References (SAP Access Control 12.0):
• Organization Rules (SP27): https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ACCESS_CONTROL/5cae1bc9a72348389e91183714220e30/8716ea4f962a2e74e10000000a44176f.html
• Creating an Organization Rule (SP10): https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ACCESS_CONTROL/5cae1bc9a72348389e91183714220e30/4fcfb0e23aa443b1e10000000a42189b.html
• Creating an Organization Rule using the Wizard (SP16): https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ACCESS_CONTROL/5cae1bc9a72348389e91183714220e30/a09276518a95115de10000000a44176d.html
• Modifying an Organization Rule (SP11): https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ACCESS_CONTROL/5cae1bc9a72348389e91183714220e30/4e61369a1ff628d4e10000000a421bc1.html
• Deleting an Organization Rule (SP25): https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ACCESS_CONTROL/5cae1bc9a72348389e91183714220e30/58bce94fb0d04a59e10000000a445394.html
• User Level Access Risk Analysis (SP10): https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ACCESS_CONTROL/5cae1bc9a72348389e91183714220e30/258ceb4fa7b94a59e10000000a445394.html
• SAP Note 3625382: Wizard 500 Internal Server Error fix
• SAP Note 1514544: Organizational Rule general guidance

entry.title: Automatic FIFO Batch Determination During Sales Order Creation in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Hi,

I would like to know if the system can automatically determine and assign the batch based on FIFO (First In, First Out) when creating a sales order, so users do not need to manually select the batch.

Thanks and regards.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Automatic FIFO Batch Determination During Sales Order...
entry.content:
Hi,

Please check the following note, especially point 6 of the resolution:

  • 3649063 - How to set up Batch Determination in Sales and Delivery scenario

In the KBA, you can find the detailed steps to configure batch determination for the sales scenario. In point 6 of the resolution, there is specific information regarding the activation of automatic batch determination for sales orders and deliveries:

  • Configuration Activity 102171: Activate Automatic Batch Determination for Sales Order Item Categories
  • Configuration Activity 102172: Activate Automatic Batch Determination for Delivery Item Categories

In these configuration activities, you must maintain the item categories for which the system should automatically determine batches when either a sales order or an outbound delivery is created.

Best regards.

entry.title: Re: Fiori List Report Issue with OData V4 VS V2
entry.content:
Hi. 

I will share what I researched tied to this topic and other people can comment for their other ideas.

Per what we know, when the Adopt Filters to add non-parameter fields to the filter bar, those new fields are visually rendering with a red asterisk (*) which means they are mandatory even though they should not be.

In SAP, and in ODATA V4 Fiori Elements, when an entity set is backed by a parametererized CDS view, the framework wraps the entity inside a navigation property path (often parsed through a technical parameter entity structure like Execute or the localized parameter container).

Because the root parameters are strictly mandatory to execute the query, the V4 smartFilterBar / FilterBar engine added linked fields or sibling fields as part of the mandatory parameter group and giving them a * even though the backend would not enforced it.

So the next steps is to override the mandatory status of those standard filter fields using local annotation (annotation.xml) in your UI5 project. The team needs to ensure those fields as optional.

The team can research on sap.ui.comp.smartfilterbar configuration properties to see if there is any specific control.

Also in Older Odata V2 apps the table personalization dialog (p13n) gives users toggles like Show Selected or Select All button. In newer odata v4 app, it only shows Deselect All button changing what is shown to the business users.

It is typically a shift in UI5 Flexibility engine used by the Odata V4 Fiori Element templates.

V4 handles column metadata asynchronously. Instead of loading every single column into memory up front to allow a massive "Select All" calculation, the V4 personalization panel assumes a lean configuration state.

The framework provides a search bar inside the table settings so users can type and find specific columns immediately. The designers replaced the old multi-state selection button with a single state Deselect All to quickly clean the slate for users who want to build a view entirely from scratch using the search bar.

So the template is functioning as designed for the V4 delivery.

If the team needs legacy personalization experience, the manifest.json may need to be reviewed under controlConfiguration and for tables, changing the table type or disablingthe default responsive table personalization back to custom dialogs may work (but research is needed). But sticking to the V4 layout is highly requested for future upgrades.

Some references: https://me.sap.com/notes/3036992 - SmartTable, Smartvariant, Smartfield, Smartchart and other Smart issues with oData V4 version - Uncaught (in promise) TypeError -> wherein It highlights that OData V4 drops the old V2 structural assumptions (sap.ui.comp) and switches to modern, asynchronous metadata paths. The changes in filter behavior and the personalization dialog are direct side effects of this library evolution.

Regards,
Mark

entry.title: Re: Simplified Git has disappeared
entry.content:
Hello.

From what we know tied to simplified git, on earlier iteration of the SAP BAS which included custom, high level wizard like Git panel designed to abstract away standard command-line Git concept for developers moving over from SAP Web IDE to BAS.

So from the above, SAP aligned with BAS standard Code OSS/VS code behaviors.
The legacy "Simplified Git" was deprecated and phased out in favor of the standard or native VS Code Source Control View.  

So sometimes when some custom SAP buttons rely on it for staging and found the panel missing on a BAS platform, the underlying Git functionality did not disappear, it just transitioned entirely to a standard industry UI panel or the integrated terminal.

The above is what we know.

Now looking at the picture where the Activity Bar/View Context Menu is shown in the SAP BAS.  Looking directly at the available views, the Simplified Git is entirely missing from the selection list. 

So it may appear that the view itself is gone from the environment.

So from the above, the extension layer is missing.  So the working theory is that it is disable or deprecated in more recent platform update across the subaccounts.

So if you do the Ctrl+SHIFT+G that may be the standard, native VS Code OSS Git interface instead.

The team can probably use your Gitlens.

So in summary:  BAS will always accept the standard Git Commands like git add, git commit and others.

The shift may be a minor bump for developers who may be looking at the old wizard style but most likely the team has to do the standard source control panel or terminal.

This is what I can tell from the above.

Regards,
Mark

entry.title: Re: Nachträgliches Ergänzen der Partnergesellschaft in be...
entry.content:
Good day @Tobias_Schlitzer 

Thank you for your question.

There is no standard job that retroactively copies the Partnergesellschaft from the customer master into already posted receivable open item. The Partnergesellschaft is determined during posting and stored in the accounting document line item. If it was missing when the receivable was posted, maintaining it later in the customer master does not automatically update the old open item. KBA 3060863 provides good details on that.

For already posted items, there is no standard mass update job that retroactively copies the Partnergesellschaft from the customer master into existing FI open items. Since the field is stored in the posted accounting document line item, the usual correction approach is to reverse and repost the document after the customer master has been corrected, or to perform a business approved reclassification/adjustment where you cannot carry out reversal. The preventive measure is for you to ensure that the Trading Partner is maintained in the customer master before posting because Trading Partner information flows to transaction data when it is maintained in the relevant intercompany customer, vendor, or G/L master data.

For future postings, the recommended solution is to prevent the issue before posting. First, adjust the customer creation interface so that the Partnergesellschaft is supplied for IC relevant customers. Second, use Manage Substitution/Validation Rules to block relevant receivable postings when the Partnergesellschaft is missing.

See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/e6175604fc874bfe84aa14d2f9ae1fbd/5c8c2825535f4e3aa9b7f716a0085221.html?locale=en-US

As a next step, test one affected customer by checking the business partner master data, posting a receivable without Partnergesellschaft, maintain the field afterward and confirm in Manage Customer Line Items that the old open item is not updated.  

Then create a validation rule in Manage Substitution/Validation Rules so that future IC relevant receivables cannot be posted without Partnergesellschaft.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: SAP Charlotte Innovation Showcase 2026 — Cross‑Industry SAP Lightning Talks
entry.content:
SAP Community Charlotte is excited to host the SAP Charlotte Innovation Showcase 2026, a virtual event featuring 15‑minute lightning talks from SAP professionals across industries.

This event is open for everyone — whether you want to present, participate, or simply learn and network with the SAP community.

The goal is to highlight innovative SAP solutions, SAP BTP architectures, AI‑enabled use cases, and real‑world transformation stories.

 

Event Curators

  • Atul Joshi — Curator & Chief Judge 1

  • Aditya Chinni — Co‑Curator & Chief Judge 2

 

Format

  • Up to 10 speakers (FIFO selection)

  • 15‑minute talk + 5‑minute Q&A

  • Cross‑industry SAP topics: SAP BTP, S/4HANA, AI, EDA, Integration Suite, Datasphere, SAC, Industry Solutions

  • 5‑member expert judging panel

  • Top 3 speakers receive the SAP Charlotte Innovator Award + Certificate

 

 

 Call for Speakers (Open to Everyone Worldwide)

We invite SAP architects, consultants, developers, and industry experts to submit proposals showcasing their SAP innovations.

Submission Requirements

  • Session title

  • 3–4 sentence abstract

  • Speaker bio

  • SAP technologies used

  • Industry focus

 

 Speaker Registration Form: https://forms.gle/LxNDnaoH4ssFMjBXA

 

 

 Participant Registration (Open for Everyone)

Join us to learn, support the speakers, and engage with the SAP community.

 

 Participant Registration Form: https://forms.gle/DZAYBn4eyn3ySe5W7

 

Certificates

All presenters will receive an official Certificate of Participation issued by the SAP Community Charlotte Curators.

entry.title: Re: Performance form custom element update from User entity via OData API upsert in CPI
entry.content: @ankitkumarpatel Do you think it could be done in Integration Center directly ? Since CPI comes at additional licence cost ? I have a customer that would like to make an entire custom section visible only for specific employees based on data that lives on their profile in EC. entry.title: How to Link Attachments Between SuccessFactors Modules Using SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
In SAP SuccessFactors integrations, there are many business scenarios where attachments need to be transferred between different modules such as Recruiting to Onboarding or Onboarding to Employee Central and other modules. One common requirement is transferring offer letter attachments from the Recruiting module to the Onboarding module during the hiring process.

Although SAP SuccessFactors provides standard OData APIs for attachment handling, implementing this requirement using the SAP SuccessFactors adapter in SAP Integration Suite involves multiple steps. Due to security and module level data separation in SAP SuccessFactors, attachments are not directly shared across modules. Instead, attachments are stored independently and linked using unique attachment IDs.

In this blog post, we will go through the complete step-by-step process of retrieving an offer letter attachment from Recruiting and linking it to the Onboarding module using the SAP SuccessFactors adapter in SAP Integration Suite.

The first step is to retrieve the offer letter attachment details from the Recruiting module in Cloud Integration using SuccessFactors adapter. Connect SuccessFactors adapter with OData V2 and retrieve the offer letter from JobApplication entity as below

Once you have the offer letter retrieved, filter for the latest offer letter if there are multiple letter attached for the same candidate. This can be done using lastModifiedDateTime/offerLetterId/createdDateTime fields to filter

Attachments cannot be directly transferred from one SuccessFactors module to another, for example from Recruiting to Onboarding, due to security and ownership restrictions within SAP SuccessFactors. If you attempt to directly reuse the same attachment ID across modules, you may encounter authorization or access rights errors because the owner and security context of the attachment in Recruiting are different from those in Onboarding.

To overcome this limitation, the attachment must first be retrieved from the source module, then uploaded again to generate a new attachment ID in the target module. Finally, the newly generated attachment ID must be linked to the employee profile in the Onboarding module.

Prepare payload to upload the attachment from Recruiting to general attachment entity in Onboarding 

Use content modifier to prepare the payload like below. Get file content, file name from Recruiting (JobApplication Entity) and store them in property to use them in the payload. The module name in Recruiting is RECRUITING and when we upload into Onboarding, it's GENERIC_OBJECT. UserId is the ID which is used to connect SuccessFactors with Integration Suite.

<Attachment>
<Attachment>
<fileContent>${property.fileContent}</fileContent>
<fileName>${property.fileName}</fileName>
<module>GENERIC_OBJECT</module>
<userId>SFAPI</userId>
<viewable>true</viewable>
</Attachment>
</Attachment>

Upload Attachment to General Attachment Entity 

Connect with SuccessFactors Adapter with request reply and use Upsert (POST) Operation to post the payload prepared in previous step to attachment entity. 

Once attachment is uploaded into generic object, store the attachmentId from the response payload 

Prepare payload to map the attachmentId to respective employee profile in Onboarding

Map the payload to target structure to upload the attachmentId

Note: We do not have to upload the file content, file name again. Instead we can link the attachmentId to employee profile in Onboarding

Upload attachmentId to MDF Object which is created in Onboarding to upload the attachments
Connect with SuccessFactors Adapter with request reply and use Upsert (POST) Operation to link the attachmentId 

Below is the response message after uploading the attachmentId successfully

<?xml version='1.0' encoding='utf-8'?><UpsertResponses><cust_ONB_NewHireDocumentsUpsertResponse><key>cust_ONB_NewHireDocuments/externalCode=100110</key><status>OK</status><editStatus>UPSERTED</editStatus><message null="true"/><index type="Edm.Int32">0</index><httpCode type="Edm.Int32">200</httpCode><inlineResults type="Bag(SFOData.UpsertResult)"/></cust_ONB_NewHireDocumentsUpsertResponse></UpsertResponses>

Reference:
Linking Attachments to an MDF Entity

entry.title: [SAC Planning] Is it realistic to operate a “Category” dimension with up to ~50,000 members created
entry.content:
1. What I would like to ask

In a SAC Planning model, we are considering a design where we create a Category dimension whose members are derived from a combination of three keys, resulting in approximately 5,000 to up to 50,000 unique keys.
Is it generally realistic/acceptable in SAC to define and operate a dimension of this size, from both a performance and operational perspective? If not recommended, what alternative design approaches would you suggest?

2. Assumptions / Environment

  • Product: SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC)
  • Model type: Planning model
  • Target dimension: Category dimension (with a hierarchy)
  • Number of members: approx. 5,000 to max. ~50,000 unique keys
  • Story consumption:
    • We do not expand or display all 50,000 members in charts/tables
    • The actual number of rows displayed in a table is around 100
  • The set of unique keys is fixed (not expected to grow over time)

3. Background (data integration scenario)

We use a Data Action to transfer data from another model (source) into the target planning model.
The target model has an additional analysis axis: the Category dimension, which does not exist in the source model.

Right after the data transfer, since Category is not provided in the source, the facts are initially posted to “Unassigned” for the Category dimension.
After that, we plan to run a Data Action to re-allocate (expand) the “Unassigned” facts into the appropriate Category members, and then delete the remaining “Unassigned” facts.

4. Proposed design (mapping approach)

  • There are three common key dimensions that exist in both the source and target models.
  • In the Category dimension, each member stores the values of these three keys as properties.
  • We are considering treating the combination of the three keys as a unique identifier, and using that combined key as the Category dimension member ID (or an equivalent key).
  • In the Data Action, we allocate “Unassigned” facts to the appropriate Category member based on the stored property values (the three keys), and then delete the “Unassigned” facts.

5. Concerns / Questions

  1. Is having a Category dimension with around 50,000 members generally within an acceptable and manageable range for SAC Planning models?
  2. If performance issues are likely, which areas are typically most impacted?
    • e.g., Data Action runtime, write/delete operations, hierarchy processing, value help/search, model saving/publishing, etc.
  3. Is a design where a 3-key combination becomes the Category member ID recommended?
    • If not, what are common alternatives in SAC (e.g., mapping via attributes, bridge-like approach, pre-aggregation, deriving category on the source side, etc.)?
  4. Are there any best practices for operations?
    • e.g., scoping Data Actions, precautions for deletions, hierarchy design, naming/ID design, etc.

6. Additional note

  • The unique keys (category candidates) are fixed and will not grow over time.
  • Users will not browse or display the full set of 50,000 members (typical display is ~100 rows).

entry.title: Re: Adaptation Project development with flp sandbox 404 e...
entry.content:
check if this helps

https://www.npmjs.com/package/@sap/ux-ui5-tooling#example-configuration-for-serving-locally-ui5

 

3. Serve Static

The serve static middleware provides the capability to serve any static resources locally from your machine. E.g. you can serve SAPUI5 locally or any other resources.

Example Configuration for serving locally SAPUI5

Pre-requisites: SAPUI5 SDK version is downloaded and extracted locally on the machine. One can download UI5 resources from https://tools.hana.ondemand.com/#sapui5

Executing npx fiori run in your project with the configuration below in a ui5.yaml file would serve the UI5 sources from your machine. Any request starting with the path parameter will be forwarded to the local path provided in the src parameter.

server:
  customMiddleware:
  - name: fiori-tools-servestatic
    afterMiddleware: compression
    configuration:
      paths:
        - path: /resources
          src: "Path/To/SAPUI5-SDK"
        - path: /test-resources
          src: "Path/To/SAPUI5-SDK"

entry.title: Designing a Portable PaPM Implementation: From Private Edition to Public Edition
entry.content:
Interest in SAP Profitability and Performance Management (PaPM) is real across many teams. Which deployment they start from tends to reflect their own priorities rather than any single requirement - for some that's data residency or internal compliance, for others audit posture, and for others simply a leadership team not yet at ease with the public cloud. For any of these reasons, SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition can be the preferred place to begin.

And then comes the question that actually matters:
If we build this on Private Edition now, how hard will it be to re-implement, or even migrate, to Public Edition later - at least from an approach perspective?

It’s a very reasonable question. The honest answer is that it depends almost entirely on the decisions you make on day one. A PaPM implementation can be designed so a future move is a controlled, mechanical exercise - or it can be designed so that a future move is effectively a second project. The difference is not the technology. It is discipline in architecture.

A bit of background: two PaPM worlds, one model
When SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud appeared, it did so alongside a mature and still-evolving on‑prem/Private Edition solution. Many teams had already built substantial content - calculations, allocations, hierarchies, complete environments - for POCs, demos, or productive use. Nobody wants to throw that away. The instinct is to preserve those environments and reuse them in the cloud, either to switch over or to explore behavior on the new platform.

SAP anticipated this. Rather than forcing a rebuild from scratch - while still guiding necessary modeling changes - PaPM ships dedicated applications for portability. The Export Environment application on the on‑prem/Private Edition side and the Content Management/Import capability on the Cloud side together make a full PaPM environment portable. The model - functions, processes, hierarchies, allocations, model/lookup tables - is treated as a self‑contained, transferable asset.

That is the foundation of this strategy. If the PaPM environment is the portable unit, then during a Private Edition implementation your job is to ensure nothing important lives outside that portable unit.


The golden rule: keep the thinking inside PaPM

Here is the single principle to adopt from day one on SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition:
Keep all data manipulation inside PaPM - not on the SAP S/4HANA side.

PaPM can do the heavy lifting - derivation, enrichment, mapping, allocation. The temptation, especially for teams with strong ABAP or HANA skills, is to “help” PaPM by preparing data upstream in S/4HANA. It can feel efficient. It’s also exactly what makes a future migration painful, because any logic you push into S/4HANA does not travel with the PaPM environment export.

So let SAP S/4HANA be a clean, boring data source. PaPM does the thinking.

Concretely, that means:

  • Prefer released standard CDS views as sources. A released view is a stable contract in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition. A custom Z‑view with embedded business logic is a dependency you must rebuild.
  • Avoid AMDP, BAdI, or exits that “pre‑shape” data. Any transformation here is invisible to PaPM and won’t survive the move.
  • No Z‑tables in S/4HANA as an interim staging layer. If you need staging or mapping, build it inside PaPM as model or lookup tables - that way it is part of the exportable environment.
  • Keep all derivation, enrichment, mapping, and allocation logic inside PaPM - via Calculation, Allocation, Model Table functions, lookups, and mappings.
  • For custom fields in S/4HANA on Private Edition, prefer Key User Extensibility (and ABAP Cloud on released objects) to remain compatible with Public Edition, which disallows classic extensibility.

The pattern is the same throughout: anything that lives in PaPM is portable; anything that lives in S/4HANA becomes custom integration work you’ll have to rebuild. Push the boundary towards PaPM as far as is sensible.

Also, even on Private Edition, favor a clean‑core approach - in‑app (Key User) extensibility and ABAP Cloud on released APIs - to reduce future rework.

Naming conventions are not cosmetic
Field naming will determine how easy your reconnection is when you point an existing PaPM environment at a Public Edition source. Migration often boils down to remapping input fields from the new source onto the existing model. That remapping is much easier - and less error‑prone - if your naming convention was consistent and intentional from the start and aligns with what exists in Public Edition.

Treat naming conventions as part of the design. Agree them before modeling starts, and write them down. It costs nothing now and saves a tedious reconciliation later.

The detail that catches teams out: schema and connection remapping
PaPM functions and model tables bind to a specific HANA database schema/connection. That binding does not automatically travel with an environment.

This is not only a Public Edition concern. It applies within your own landscape - every time you promote an environment across Dev -> Test -> Prod. If you move environments manually between instances, you must adjust the schema/connection in the imported environment; it won’t point itself to the right place. Skip this, and the first run fails against non‑existent data.

For PaPM - Standard Model schema located in this file:

Practical takeaway: make schema and connection remapping a standard, documented step in your promotion checklist, not something to rely on memory for. If you followed the golden rule and kept naming consistent, schema/connection remapping is typically the only manual touch required - mechanical, repeatable, and quick once it’s a defined routine.

Post‑import checklist (make it explicit):

  • Update destinations/connections to SAP S/4HANA sources.
  • Rebind HANA schema and technical users where required.
  • Run data access smoke tests (queries/lookups) before executing processes.

Function parity: a quick sanity check
The PaPM model is portable, but always do a brief function‑parity check to confirm the specific PaPM functions you rely on exist or have equivalents in PaPM Cloud. This is typically straightforward, but a 5‑minute review per project de‑risks surprises.

What a future migration actually looks like
If the implementation is designed this way, a future move to SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition stops being a redesign and becomes a re‑import.

In practice, the steps are:

  1. Export the PaPM environment.
  2. Import it into the PaPM Cloud tenant.
  3. Reconnect source systems - now SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition instead of Private Edition.
  4. Remap input fields to the model (helped by your naming convention and released CDS usage).
  5. Rebind schema/connection and validate access.
  6. Run.

The integration layer is re‑pointed; the logic layer is preserved. This lets customers start on Private Edition today, for whatever security reasons apply, without silently committing to a second implementation when they are ready for Public Edition.

In short
For a customer who wants to begin on SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition but keep the Public Edition option genuinely open:

  • Treat the PaPM environment as the portable unit; use Export Environment/Content Management for the transfer.
  • Keep derivation, enrichment, mapping, and allocations inside PaPM. Let SAP S/4HANA be a clean data source.
  • Prefer released CDS views as sources; avoid Z‑views with embedded logic, AMDP/BAdI/exits, and Z‑table staging layers.
  • For custom fields, use Key User Extensibility (and ABAP Cloud on released objects) to align with Public Edition.
  • Agree and document a deliberate field naming convention up front.
  • Build schema/connection remapping into your promotion checklist - for Dev/Test/Prod today, and for Public Edition tomorrow.
  • Do a quick PaPM function‑parity check during design.

Get these right and “migrate to Public Edition” becomes a planned, low‑drama exercise rather than a project of its own.

I hope my experience will help you in future implementation projects.
Many thanks for your attention, all the best.

Below you'll find a link to a related topic on migration and development alignment:
SAP Group Reporting Implementation: Architecture and Methodology for Success 

Let`s keep in touch:
https://www.linkedin.com/in/eugene-kavaleu/

entry.title: Purchase Requisition generated for non-finalized Sales Order
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are working w S/4HANA Public Cloud and I'd like to ask your support, thoughts.

When a Sales Order is created for a Purchased Components (e.g. Trading Goods) and it is saved but not yet finalized, SAP generates Purchase Requisitions immediately. PRs are then accessable for Purchasing Team to convert them to Purchase Orders, while Sales Order content can still change. We work with automatic PR approval workflow for system generated PRs (MRP/SO) and would like to stick to that concept.

How to trigger PR creation only when a Sales Order is finalized (e.g. document sent status at Sales Order Header level)?

Thank you for your support, Krisztina

entry.title: WebGUI Short Dump GETWA_NOT_ASSIGNED in CL_HTTP_EXT_ITS_2
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I am facing an issue with SAP WebGUI.

When trying to log in to WebGUI, the browser shows HTTP 404 error and in ST22 I found the following dump:

Category: ABAP programming error
Runtime Errors: GETWA_NOT_ASSIGNED
ABAP Program: CL_HTTP_EXT_ITS_2=============CP
Application Component: BC-FES-ITS

Short Text:
Field symbol has not been assigned yet.

Error Analysis:
An attempt was made to access a field symbol that has not been assigned yet (data segment number "-1").
Interesting behavior:

  • Yesterday my account could access WebGUI normally without any issue.
  • Starting today, my account can no longer log in to WebGUI and triggers the dump above.
  • My friend can still log in to WebGUI normally using his own account on his laptop.
  • However, when I tried logging in using my account on my friend's laptop, the same dump/error occurred.
  • After my account triggered the error on his laptop, even my friend’s own account could no longer access WebGUI on that same laptop/browser session.
  • I also still cannot access WebGUI from my own laptop.
  • SAP GUI desktop login still works normally.
  • Clearing cache/cookies and using incognito mode does not solve the issue.

    Additional information:
    • My account is a super user with roles SAP_ALL and SAP_NEW.
    • The issue does not seem related to authorization because SAP GUI desktop login still works normally.

Has anyone experienced something similar with BC-FES-ITS or CL_HTTP_EXT_ITS_2 dumps?

Could this be related to:

  • ITS session corruption

  • WebGUI personalization

  • role/menu rendering

  • kernel or SAP Note issue?

Any suggestions on what to check next would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Submit and return silent skip in process inbound api
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

I have an issue sometimes data from the inbound REST API silently skips the process. When this happens, no data is processed, and absolutely no log is written into our custom logging table. Since this issue only happens occasionally in Production (and works fine in Development/Quality environments), I suspect it might be related to system load or session limitations. The submit program originally waiting time with default 12 second the code look like this.

  SUBMIT zxxx WITH v_wait = 12
              AND RETURN.

Any insights, experiences, or suggestions would be highly appreciated.

Thank you in advance!

entry.title: debugging software that connects to SAP ASE
entry.content:
Hi,

I'm trying to run gdb on my software that should connect to SAP ASE.

Both the server (version 16.1) and my software located on Linux.

 

So this is my scenario:

1. Laptop booted up.

2. Server starts up.

3. I ran gdb on my program.

4.. I am able to run it successfully until I find the problem.

5.. When I hit the problem I quit the gdb immediately and go to fix the issue.

6. After that I recompile and start gdb again.

7. At that point the only thing I can do is connect to the server. No query can be executed. The program just stuck somewhere inside the server.

 

Does this sounds familiar to anybody?

 

The server is started with the following script:

 

[code]

 

ulimit -s unlimited
cd /opt/sap
su
source SYBASE.sh
. /opt/sap/SYBASE.sh
cd ASE-16_1/install
startserver -f RUN_REINCLOUD

[/code]

Is there any way to continue debugging on the second run of my program?

 

When I interrupted the second gdb session I get the following gdb backtrace:

[code]

[Thread 0x7fffeaa7e640 (LWP 11506) exited]
^C
Thread 1 "dbhandler" received signal SIGINT, Interrupt.
0x00007ffff5ac4f6f in poll () from /lib64/libc.so.6

entry.title: Get Certified: SAP Certified – Data Architect – SAP Business Data Cloud | Live Session Series
entry.content:
Get ready to earn your SAP Certified – Data Architect – SAP Business Data Cloud (C_BDCDA) certification. We’re here to help you get there.

Our Get Certified live session series is designed to guide you through your certification journey with the support of the certification developers and subject matter experts. This live session series will give you the knowledge, confidence, and preparation you need to become a certified SAP Data Architect.

What’s Included in the Series?

This series consists of two focused sessions that you can register for as a bundle:

📌Kick-Off Session (1 hour): July 9, Thursday, 09:00 - 10:00 CEST

Get started with a comprehensive introduction to the certification:

  • Overview of the live session series and what to expect
  • Introduction to the C_BDCDA certification — scope, format, and exam interface
  • Tips and strategies for effective preparation
  • Walkthrough of the learning journey and self-study content
  • Guidance on how the content review session connects to your study plan

📌Content Review Session (2 hours): July 16, Thursday, 09:00 – 11:00 CEST

Go deeper into the topics that matter most for your exam:

  • Coverage of certification-relevant topics from both Becoming an SAP Data Architect (BDCDA1) – All Units and Mastering SAP Data Architecture (BDCDA2) – All Units
  • Expert explanations of key concepts
  • The opportunity to ask questions and clarify areas where you need additional support
  • A valuable complement to your self-study that helps bridge knowledge gaps

What Will You Walk Away With?

After participating in this live session series, you will:

  • ✅Understand the format and scope of the roleplay certification for C_BDCDA certification
  • ✅Feel confident navigating the Yoodli interface
  • ✅Have clarity on the most important topics to focus on
  • ✅Be ready to attempt the certification

Who Is This For?

This series is perfect for:

  • Professionals preparing for the C_BDCDA certification
  • Data architects working with SAP Business Data Cloud
  • SAP project team members looking to validate their technical knowledge
  • Anyone who has started the self-study learning journey and wants expert guidance to reinforce their preparation

Register Now: Get Certified: SAP Certified - Data Architect - SAP Business Data Cloud

entry.content:
How to reset an S‑user password?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

entry.title: Re: How to reset an S‑user password?
entry.content:
1.Go to Forgot My Password

2.Enter S-user ID S00XXXXXXXX  

3.Click Continue.

4. An e-mail will be sent from SAP ID Service (ias@notifications.sap.com) to the e-mail address registered for the user. 

5.Open the e-mail to find the password reset link.

More detail on how to reset an S-user password can be found in 1808560 - How to reset an S-user ID password - SAP for Me

Best regards,
SAP Support
